The top few teams may be [promoted](/wiki/Promotion_and_relegation "Promotion and relegation") to a higher division, and one or more of the teams finishing at the bottom are [relegated](/wiki/Promotion_and_relegation "Promotion and relegation") to a lower division.[[184]](#cite_note-190)

Some exceptions to this system occur in the [New World](/wiki/New_World "New World") (Americas) and Australia:

* The majority of the Latin American leagues (including [Liga MX](/wiki/Liga_MX "Liga MX") from Mexico and [Liga Profesional de Fútbol](/wiki/Argentine_Primera_Divisi%C3%B3n "Argentine Primera División") from Argentina) divide football championships into two sections named [Apertura and Clausura](/wiki/Apertura_and_Clausura "Apertura and Clausura") (Spanish for *Opening* and *Closing*), awarding a champion for each.[[185]](#cite_note-191) After a first phase using points in a round-robin tournament, these leagues usually have a knockout phase, with a final deciding the champion of each Apertura and Clausura tournament. In some countries, such as Uruguay,[[186]](#cite_note-192) the winners of the Apertura and Clausura play each other in a play-off for the season title.
* The [Brazilian Série A](/wiki/Campeonato_Brasileiro_S%C3%A9rie_A "Campeonato Brasileiro Série A") ("Brasileirão") uses a single-season double round-robin format to determine the champion, similar to most European leagues, although it is played from April or May to December.[[187]](#cite_note-193) Brazilian clubs also participate in [state leagues](/wiki/State_football_leagues_in_Brazil "State football leagues in Brazil") from January to April, and at least one state league, the [Campeonato Carioca](/wiki/Campeonato_Carioca "Campeonato Carioca") in [Rio de Janeiro](/wiki/Rio_de_Janeiro_(state) "Rio de Janeiro (state)"), had a [split season](/wiki/Split_season "Split season") format until 2020.[[188]](#cite_note-194) However, Brazil has never used an Apertura and Clausura system in its national tournament, instead employing various formats with play-offs until 2002.[[189]](#cite_note-195)
* [Major League Soccer](/wiki/Major_League_Soccer "Major League Soccer") in the United States and Canada uses a closed league system (without promotion and relegation) and a conference system, similar to that used in other sports leagues in both countries. In each conference, clubs play a round-robin schedule during the regular season, and those with the most points qualify for the play-offs, which conclude with a final to determine the champion.[[190]](#cite_note-196)
* The [A-League](/wiki/A-League_Men "A-League Men") in Australia also uses a closed league system. At the end of a regular season played in a round-robin format, the clubs with the most points compete in play-offs to determine the league champion.[[191]](#cite_note-197)

The teams finishing at the top of a country's league may also be eligible to play in [international club competitions](/wiki/List_of_association_football_competitions "List of association football competitions") in the following season. Most countries supplement the league system with one or more "cup" competitions organised on a [knock-out](/wiki/Single_elimination_tournament "Single elimination tournament") basis. These include the [domestic cup](/wiki/Domestic_cup "Domestic cup"), which may be open to all eligible teams in a country's league system—both professional and amateur—and is organised by the national federation.[[192]](#cite_note-198)

Some countries' top divisions feature highly-paid star players; in smaller countries, lower divisions, and many women's clubs, players may be part-timers with a second job, or amateurs. The top five European leagues – [Premier League](/wiki/Premier_League "Premier League") (England),[[193]](#cite_note-199) [Bundesliga](/wiki/Bundesliga "Bundesliga") (Germany), [La Liga](/wiki/La_Liga "La Liga") (Spain), [Serie A](/wiki/Serie_A "Serie A") (Italy), and [Ligue 1](/wiki/Ligue_1 "Ligue 1") (France) – attract most of the world's best players and, during the 2006–07 season, each of these leagues had a total wage cost in excess of [€](/wiki/Euro "Euro")600 million.[[194]](#cite_note-200) These leagues also generated a combined €17.2 billion in revenue in the 2021–22 season from television contracts, matchday tickets, sponsorships, and other sources.[[195]](#cite_note-201) In the 2022–23 season, clubs in Europe's "[big five](/wiki/Big_Five_(association_football) "Big Five (association football)")" leagues had an aggregate wage cost of €13 billion, with each league spending more than €1.8 billion in wages. In contrast, the combined revenue of these leagues was €19.6 billion.[[196]](#cite_note-202)

## Variants and casual play

See also: [List of types of football](/wiki/List_of_types_of_football "List of types of football")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/36/Street_soccer_in_Lagos.jpg/250px-Street_soccer_in_Lagos.jpg)](/wiki/File:Street_soccer_in_Lagos.jpg)

Street play in [Lagos](/wiki/Lagos "Lagos"), Nigeria

Variants of association football have been codified for teams of reduced size, such as [five-a-side football](/wiki/Five-a-side_football "Five-a-side football"); for non-traditional playing environments, including [beach soccer](/wiki/Beach_soccer "Beach soccer"), [futsal](/wiki/Futsal "Futsal") and [indoor soccer](/wiki/Indoor_soccer "Indoor soccer"); and for athletes with disabilities ([Paralympic football](/wiki/Paralympic_football "Paralympic football")).

[Street football](/wiki/Street_football_(association_football) "Street football (association football)") can be played with only minimal equipment – a basic game can be played on almost any open area of reasonable size with just a ball made from almost any material and items to mark the positions of the goalposts.[[197]](#cite_note-203)[[198]](#cite_note-204) Such informal games can have team sizes that vary from eleven-a-side, can use a limited or modified subset of the official rules, and can be self-officiated by the players.[[199]](#cite_note-205)
